beats.prompt: Warm up: match each idea to its answer.

feedback.correct: Right. Today: when a zero-sum game has no saddle point, both players must mix ‚Äî and von Neumann proved every finite zero-sum game has a minimax value.
hints: If there's no pure Nash equilibrium, you must randomize to prevent exploitation.
Mixed strategies are calibrated so the opponent gains nothing by deviating ‚Äî you leave them indifferent.
Matching Pennies: each player mixes 50/50; the value is 0.

beats.prompt: Zero-sum game WITH a saddle point ‚Äî should you randomize?

No, play the saddle.note: Correct. A saddle point is simultaneously the row-minimum and column-maximum ‚Äî neither player can gain by deviating, so pure play is already optimal. Mixing away from it can only hurt you.

Yes, always mix.note: Only mix when there is no saddle. A saddle is the deterministic value of the game ‚Äî mixing away from it cannot improve your guarantee and may reduce it.

Depends on luck.note: The value of the game is deterministic, not random. At a saddle point, neither player benefits from randomizing regardless of what the opponent does.

interaction.title: Maximin, minimax & value
interaction.body: **Maximin** (Row): for each row, find its worst-case payoff (the row minimum); then pick the row with the largest such minimum. This is the most Row can guarantee. **Minimax** (Col): for each column, find the best-case payoff for Row (the column maximum); then pick the column with the smallest such maximum. This is the least Col can hold Row to. **Value** V: when maximin = minimax, that common number is the game's value ‚Äî a saddle point exists and pure play is optimal. Von Neumann's minimax theorem (1928) guarantees every finite zero-sum game has a value V, possibly requiring mixed strategies.

feedback.correct: Keep the two worst-case perspectives in mind: Row secures ‚â•V, Col holds Row to ‚â§V ‚Äî they meet exactly at V.
hints: Maximin (Row): take the minimum of each row, then the maximum over rows.
Minimax (Col): take the maximum of each column, then the minimum over columns.
When maximin = minimax, that shared number is the saddle-point value V.

beats.prompt: Row's payoffs are shown (Col's = their negatives). Find the saddle point ‚Äî the cell that is the minimum of its row AND the maximum of its column. What is the value of this game?

feedback.correct: Saddle at (Row A, Col A): Row A's row-minimum is 3 (‚â§5), and Col A's column-maximum is 3 (‚â•2). Maximin = minimax = 3. Row plays Row A; Col plays Col A.
hints: Row minimums: Row A ‚Üí min(3,5)=3, Row B ‚Üí min(2,4)=2. Maximin = max(3,2) = 3.
Column maximums: Col A ‚Üí max(3,2)=3, Col B ‚Üí max(5,4)=5. Minimax = min(3,5) = 3.
Maximin = minimax = 3 ‚Üí saddle at (Row A, Col A). Value = 3.

hero.structuralReadout: Row-min of Row A = 3 = Col-max of Col A ‚Üí saddle at (Row A, Col A), value = 3.

beats.prompt: Another saddle-point game. Scan row-minimums and column-maximums ‚Äî where do they meet?

feedback.correct: Saddle at (Row A, Col A): row-min of Row A = min(4,6) = 4; col-max of Col A = max(4,3) = 4. Maximin = minimax = 4. Value = 4.
hints: Row minimums: Row A ‚Üí min(4,6)=4, Row B ‚Üí min(3,5)=3. Maximin = 4.
Column maximums: Col A ‚Üí max(4,3)=4, Col B ‚Üí max(6,5)=6. Minimax = 4.
They agree at 4 ‚Üí saddle at (Row A, Col A), value = 4.

beats.prompt: This game has NO saddle point ‚Äî verify that, then find the mixed-strategy value using v = (ad ‚àí bc)/(a + d ‚àí b ‚àí c), where a, b, c, d are the row payoffs reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

feedback.correct: No saddle: maximin = max(1,2) = 2; minimax = min(4,3) = 3; 2 ‚âÝ 3. Formula: Œî = 1+2‚àí3‚àí4 = ‚àí4; v = (1¬∑2 ‚àí 3¬∑4)/(‚àí4) = ‚àí10/(‚àí4) = 5/2. Row plays Top with p = 1/2; Col plays Left with q = 1/4.
hints: Check: row-mins are min(1,3)=1 and min(4,2)=2; col-maxes are max(1,4)=4 and max(3,2)=3. Maximin=2 ‚âÝ Minimax=3 ‚Üí no saddle.
Apply v = (ad‚àíbc)/(a+d‚àíb‚àíc) with a=1, b=3, c=4, d=2: Œî = 1+2‚àí3‚àí4 = ‚àí4.
v = (1¬∑2 ‚àí 3¬∑4)/(‚àí4) = (2‚àí12)/(‚àí4) = 5/2. Row's top-row probability: p = (d‚àíc)/Œî = (2‚àí4)/(‚àí4) = 1/2.

hero.structuralReadout: No saddle ‚Üí 2√ó2 formula: v = (ad‚àíbc)/(a+d‚àíb‚àíc) = 5/2; Row plays Top with p = 1/2.

beats.prompt: Three lenses on the minimax theorem. Predict the stable value, then reveal each perspective.

lenses.label: Maximin
lenses.body: Row chooses the strategy that maximizes the worst-case payoff ‚Äî guaranteeing at least V no matter what Col does. This is the best Row can secure against an adversary.

lenses.label: Minimax
lenses.body: Col chooses the strategy that minimizes the best-case payoff for Row ‚Äî holding Row to at most V no matter what Row does. This is the tightest ceiling Col can enforce.

lenses.label: Minimax theorem
lenses.body: Von Neumann (1928): in every finite zero-sum game, maximin = minimax = V. The Nash equilibrium in zero-sum games is exactly the minimax/maximin solution ‚Äî a unique value both sides can independently enforce.

feedback.correct: Row secures ‚â•V, Col limits Row to ‚â§V, and von Neumann proved these always meet at V. In zero-sum games, Nash equilibrium ‚â° minimax ‚â° maximin ‚Äî one theorem, three perspectives.
hints: Maximin: scan each row's minimum, pick the row with the largest minimum.
Minimax: scan each column's maximum, pick the column with the smallest maximum.
Von Neumann proved floor and ceiling always coincide for finite zero-sum games.

beats.interviewNote: Von Neumann's minimax theorem (1928) is the bedrock of zero-sum game theory ‚Äî every finite zero-sum game has a value. Interview angle: always scan for a saddle point first (O(mn)); if none, use the 2√ó2 formula; for larger games, set up a linear program. Two-finger Morra is a classic desk problem: the negative value means Col (the 'even-sum' player in this framing) holds a slight structural advantage.
beats.prompt: Two-finger Morra: Row wins if the total fingers differ; Col wins if they match. Row's payoff matrix is [[2, ‚àí3], [‚àí3, 4]] (Row shows 1 or 2 fingers; Col shows 1 or 2). What is the value of this game?

feedback.correct: No saddle (row-mins ‚àí3, ‚àí3; col-maxes 2, 4 ‚Üí 2 ‚âÝ ‚àí3). Œî = 2+4‚àí(‚àí3)‚àí(‚àí3) = 12; v = (2¬∑4 ‚àí (‚àí3)(‚àí3))/12 = (8‚àí9)/12 = ‚àí1/12. The game slightly favors Col.
hints: Check for a saddle: row-mins are min(2,‚àí3)=‚àí3 and min(‚àí3,4)=‚àí3; col-maxes are max(2,‚àí3)=2 and max(‚àí3,4)=4. ‚àí3 ‚âÝ 2 ‚Üí no saddle.

beats.prompt: Lock in the formula. Same no-saddle game as the explore beat: row payoffs [[1, 3], [4, 2]].

interaction.scenario: Zero-sum game, row payoffs \(M = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 3 \\ 4 & 2\end{pmatrix}\). There is no saddle point. Use the 2√ó2 mixed-value formula \(v = \dfrac{ad - bc}{a + d - b - c}\) and the row-probability formula \(p = \dfrac{d - c}{a + d - b - c}\).

fields.label: Row's probability on the TOP row

hints: Compute \(\Delta = a+d-b-c = 1+2-3-4 = -4\).

feedback.correct: Every finite zero-sum game has a value V ‚Äî the most Row can guarantee and the least Col can hold Row to. If a saddle point exists (row-min = col-max at some cell), play it pure. If not, both players randomize: use v = (ad‚àíbc)/(a+d‚àíb‚àíc) for 2√ó2 games. Next: games that unfold over time ‚Äî backward induction (L5).
hints: Saddle point: row-minimum = column-maximum ‚Üí pure optimal play, value = that number.
No saddle: v = (ad‚àíbc)/(a+d‚àíb‚àíc) for the 2√ó2 mixed value.
Von Neumann's minimax theorem: maximin = minimax = V for all finite zero-sum games.
